concepts.name: Tokenization
concepts.description: Splitting text into terms: lowercasing, stemming, stop words, n-grams.
concepts.mentalModel: Tokenization decides what counts as a 'word' for search. The same analyzer must run at index time and query time, or the query term will never match the indexed term.
commonMistakes: Using different analyzers for indexing and querying
Stemming too aggressively so distinct words collide
Dropping stop words that carry meaning in short queries
concepts.realWorldUsage: Elasticsearch/Lucene analyzers, Tantivy tokenizers, every lexical search engine.

concepts.name: Inverted Index
concepts.description: Term → posting list mapping that powers fast keyword lookup.
concepts.mentalModel: An inverted index flips documents inside out: instead of doc → terms, you store term → list of docs (postings). Query answering becomes intersecting/merging sorted posting lists.
commonMistakes: Storing postings unsorted, making intersection O(n*m)
Forgetting document frequency, which ranking later needs
Not separating the dictionary from the postings on disk
concepts.realWorldUsage: Lucene, Tantivy, PostgreSQL GIN indexes, every full-text search system.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — The term vocabulary & postings lists

concepts.name: TF-IDF
concepts.description: Term frequency × inverse document frequency weighting for relevance.
concepts.mentalModel: TF-IDF says a term matters more when it appears often in a document (TF) but is rare across the corpus (IDF). It is the conceptual ancestor of BM25.
commonMistakes: Ignoring document length so long docs always win
Using raw counts instead of dampened term frequency
concepts.realWorldUsage: Baseline ranking, feature input to learning-to-rank models.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — Scoring & term weighting

concepts.name: BM25
concepts.description: The standard lexical ranking function: TF saturation + IDF + length normalization.
concepts.mentalModel: BM25 fixes TF-IDF's two flaws: term frequency saturates (the 10th occurrence adds little via the k1 parameter) and document length is normalized (the b parameter). It is still the strongest single keyword ranker and the lexical half of hybrid search.
commonMistakes: Believing embeddings make BM25 obsolete — they are complementary
Leaving k1/b at defaults without tuning on your corpus
Skipping document length normalization
Not building search evals to compare against vector retrieval
concepts.realWorldUsage: Default scorer in Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Lucene, Tantivy; lexical leg of hybrid retrieval.

concepts.name: Ranking Metrics
concepts.description: Precision, recall, MRR, and nDCG for measuring retrieval quality.
concepts.mentalModel: Precision/recall measure set quality; MRR rewards getting one right answer near the top; nDCG rewards graded relevance discounted by rank. Pick the metric that matches what users feel.
commonMistakes: Reporting accuracy instead of rank-aware metrics
Using nDCG without graded relevance labels
Measuring recall without a fixed cutoff k
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every search/recommendation quality dashboard.

resources.title: Stanford IR Book — Evaluation in IR

concepts.name: Search Evals
concepts.description: Building a labelled query set and harness to compare retrieval approaches.
concepts.mentalModel: You cannot improve retrieval you cannot measure. A search eval is a fixed set of queries with known-good results plus a harness that scores any retriever — it turns ranking changes from vibes into numbers.
commonMistakes: Tuning ranking without a frozen eval set
Letting the eval set leak into tuning decisions
Too few queries to detect real regressions
concepts.realWorldUsage: BEIR benchmark, internal relevance suites at every search company.

concepts.name: Hybrid Search
concepts.description: Fusing lexical (BM25) and vector retrieval, usually via reciprocal rank fusion.
concepts.mentalModel: Lexical search nails exact terms and rare tokens; vector search captures meaning. Hybrid search runs both and fuses the rankings (commonly Reciprocal Rank Fusion) so you get precision and recall together.
commonMistakes: Naively averaging incomparable BM25 and cosine scores
Skipping RRF and instead picking one retriever per query
Not re-evaluating after adding the second retriever
concepts.realWorldUsage: Production RAG retrieval, Elasticsearch hybrid, Weaviate/Qdrant hybrid modes.

concepts.name: Reranking
concepts.description: A second-stage cross-encoder pass that reorders the top-k candidates.
concepts.mentalModel: Retrieval is recall-oriented and cheap; reranking is precision-oriented and expensive. Retrieve ~100 candidates fast, then run a heavier cross-encoder on just those to reorder the top 10.
commonMistakes: Reranking the whole corpus instead of a candidate set
Adding a reranker without measuring latency budget
Not checking that reranking actually beats the retriever on evals
concepts.realWorldUsage: Cohere Rerank, cross-encoder rerankers in RAG pipelines.

concepts.name: Query Rewriting
concepts.description: Expanding, correcting, and normalizing queries before retrieval.
concepts.mentalModel: Query rewriting cleans up what the user typed before you look it up in the index: fix spelling, expand synonyms, drop stop words, classify intent. Doing this once up front is what makes the rest of the pipeline work — never trust the raw query.

concepts.name: Search Systems
concepts.description: Inverted index, ranking, autocomplete at scale.
concepts.mentalModel: Discovery search is for "I do not know what I want yet" — no real query, just signals like recency, popularity, personalization, and browseable categories. It has a different ranking function and a different evaluation than keyword search; do not measure it the same way.

concepts.name: Embeddings
concepts.description: Mapping text/images into dense vectors where distance encodes meaning.
concepts.mentalModel: An embedding model projects content into a space where 'close' means 'semantically similar'. Search becomes geometry: find the nearest vectors to the query vector.
commonMistakes: Comparing vectors from two different embedding models
Forgetting to normalize when using cosine similarity
Ignoring the model's max input length and silently truncating
concepts.realWorldUsage: OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage embedding APIs, semantic search, RAG retrieval.

concepts.name: Vector Similarity
concepts.description: Cosine, dot product, and L2 distance — how to score vector closeness.
concepts.mentalModel: Cosine measures angle (direction), dot product measures angle and magnitude, L2 measures straight-line distance. On normalized vectors cosine and dot product rank identically, and L2 becomes a monotonic function of them.
commonMistakes: Mixing distance metrics between indexing and querying
Using dot product on un-normalized vectors and being surprised by magnitude bias
Assuming the metric does not matter for the chosen index
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every vector index exposes a metric choice; it must match the embedding model.

concepts.name: Top-k Vector Search
concepts.description: Returning the k nearest vectors to a query, exact or approximate.
concepts.mentalModel: Top-k is a bounded selection problem: maintain a size-k min-heap of best candidates while scanning. Exact search scans everything; ANN scans a clever subset.
commonMistakes: Sorting all scores instead of using a k-sized heap
Confusing recall@k (quality) with k (result count)

concepts.name: Brute-Force Vector DB
concepts.description: Exact nearest-neighbour search by scanning every vector — the correctness baseline.
concepts.mentalModel: Brute force computes the metric against every stored vector. It is O(n·d) and slow at scale, but it is exact — making it the recall=1.0 baseline that every ANN index is measured against.
commonMistakes: Skipping the brute-force baseline, so ANN recall is unmeasurable
Not batching the matrix multiply for the scan
Assuming brute force is always too slow — it is fine for <100k vectors
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IndexFlat, the ground-truth oracle for ANN benchmarks.

concepts.name: HNSW
concepts.description: Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs — the dominant ANN index.
concepts.mentalModel: HNSW builds a layered proximity graph: sparse upper layers for long hops, dense lower layers for fine search. A query greedily walks toward the nearest neighbour, descending layers — turning O(n) scan into O(log n) graph traversal.
commonMistakes: Tuning efSearch/efConstruction blindly without a recall curve
Ignoring that HNSW is memory-hungry and hard to update/delete
Expecting exact results — it is approximate by design
concepts.realWorldUsage: Default index in Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector, Lucene/Elasticsearch kNN.

resources.title: Efficient and robust ANN using HNSW graphs (Malkov & Yashunin)

concepts.name: IVF (Inverted File)
concepts.description: Cluster vectors with k-means, then search only the nearest cells (nprobe).
concepts.mentalModel: IVF partitions the space into Voronoi cells via k-means. A query is matched to its nearest cells (nprobe of them) and only those vectors are scanned — trading recall for a big speedup.
commonMistakes: Setting nprobe=1 and wondering why recall is poor
Too few or too many centroids for the dataset size
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IVF indexes, often combined with product quantization (IVF-PQ).

concepts.name: Product Quantization
concepts.description: Compress vectors into sub-space codebooks for tiny memory footprint.
concepts.mentalModel: PQ splits each vector into m sub-vectors and replaces each with the id of its nearest centroid in a per-sub-space codebook. A 1536-d float32 vector (6KB) shrinks to a handful of bytes, at the cost of approximate distances.
commonMistakes: Using PQ without IVF and losing the speedup
Too-aggressive compression that destroys recall
concepts.realWorldUsage: FAISS IVF-PQ, billion-scale vector search under a memory budget.

resources.title: Product Quantization for Nearest Neighbor Search (Jégou et al.)

concepts.name: Metadata Filtering
concepts.description: Combining vector search with structured predicates (tenant, date, tags).
concepts.mentalModel: Real queries are 'nearest vectors WHERE tenant=X AND date>Y'. Pre-filtering shrinks the candidate set before ANN (accurate, can be slow); post-filtering runs ANN then drops misses (fast, can return too few). The hard part is doing it inside a graph index.
commonMistakes: Post-filtering an HNSW result and returning fewer than k items
Treating filters as an afterthought to the index design
Ignoring multi-tenancy isolation in a shared index
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every production vector DB; critical for multi-tenant RAG.

concepts.name: Recall / Latency Tradeoffs
concepts.description: Reading recall-vs-latency curves to choose ANN parameters.
concepts.mentalModel: Every ANN knob trades recall for speed/memory. The honest artifact is a curve: recall on the y-axis, latency (or QPS, or memory) on the x-axis, with brute force as the recall=1.0 anchor.
commonMistakes: Reporting a single recall number with no latency context
Comparing indexes at different recall operating points
concepts.realWorldUsage: ann-benchmarks.com, every vector DB selection decision.

concepts.name: RAG
concepts.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: ground an LLM answer in retrieved context.
concepts.mentalModel: RAG = retrieve relevant chunks, pack them into the prompt, then generate. The quality ceiling is set by retrieval — a perfect LLM cannot answer from context it never received. Most 'RAG is bad' problems are retrieval problems.
commonMistakes: Blaming the LLM when retrieval failed
No eval set, so retrieval changes are guesswork
Stuffing too many chunks and burying the answer
Skipping reranking and hybrid retrieval
concepts.realWorldUsage: Doc Q&A, support bots, code assistants, HighSignal's intelligence engine.

concepts.name: Chunking
concepts.description: Splitting documents into retrievable units that preserve meaning.
concepts.mentalModel: A chunk is the atomic unit of retrieval — too big and embeddings blur, too small and context is lost. Good chunking respects structure (headings, functions, paragraphs) and adds overlap so answers are not cut in half.
commonMistakes: Fixed character splits that cut sentences mid-thought
Zero overlap, so boundary-spanning answers are lost
One chunk strategy for every document type
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every RAG ingestion pipeline.

concepts.name: Context Packing
concepts.description: Ordering and budgeting retrieved context within the model's window.
concepts.mentalModel: Context is a budget. Models attend unevenly (lost-in-the-middle), so order matters: put the strongest evidence at the edges, dedupe, and cut before you overflow.
commonMistakes: Filling the whole window because it is available
Ignoring position effects on attention

resources.title: Lost in the Middle (Liu et al.)

concepts.name: Structured Outputs
concepts.description: Forcing LLM output into a validated JSON schema.
concepts.mentalModel: Free text is unparseable glue between systems. Structured outputs constrain generation to a schema (via grammar/JSON mode) so the model's answer is a typed object your code can trust — or reject.
commonMistakes: Parsing JSON out of prose with regex instead of schema mode
No validation step, so malformed output crashes downstream
Over-nesting schemas the model cannot reliably fill
concepts.realWorldUsage: Function arguments, extraction pipelines, agent state, every LLM-to-code boundary.

concepts.name: Tool Calling
concepts.description: Letting an LLM invoke functions/APIs via structured calls.
concepts.mentalModel: Tool calling turns the LLM into a planner: it emits a structured request to a named tool, your code executes it, and the result goes back into context. The model never runs code — it decides what to run.
commonMistakes: Vague tool descriptions, so the model picks the wrong tool
No error handling for failed tool calls
Letting tools take dangerous actions without approval
concepts.realWorldUsage: MCP servers, agent frameworks, Claude/OpenAI tool use.

concepts.name: Agent Loops
concepts.description: The plan → act → observe loop, with memory and stopping conditions.
concepts.mentalModel: An agent is a loop: the model proposes an action, a tool executes it, the observation re-enters context, repeat until done. The engineering is in the guardrails — step limits, memory, and human approval — not the prompt.
commonMistakes: No step limit, so the loop runs away
Letting context grow unbounded across iterations
No human approval gate for irreversible actions
concepts.realWorldUsage: Coding agents, research agents, Aliveville's agent world.

concepts.name: Model Routing
concepts.description: Sending each request to the cheapest model that can handle it.
concepts.mentalModel: Not every request needs the frontier model. A router classifies difficulty and dispatches: small/cheap model for easy calls, large model for hard ones — with cost and latency tracked per route.
commonMistakes: Routing without evals, so quality silently drops
No fallback when the cheap model fails
concepts.realWorldUsage: Cost optimization in production LLM apps, the free-AI-first principle.

concepts.name: Prompt & Version Logging
concepts.description: Treating prompts as versioned artifacts with logged inputs/outputs.
concepts.mentalModel: Treat prompts like code — every production change gets a version, a saved diff, and a re-run of your evals. Without that, when quality drops you have no commit to point at.

concepts.name: LLM Evals
concepts.description: Measuring LLM output quality with datasets, graders, and LLM-as-judge.
concepts.mentalModel: Evals are unit tests for non-deterministic systems. Fix a dataset of inputs with expected behavior, define a grader (exact match, rubric, or LLM-as-judge), and run it on every prompt/model change.
commonMistakes: Shipping prompt changes on vibes with no eval
LLM-as-judge without checking the judge against humans
Eval set that does not cover real failure modes
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every serious LLM product; regression gates in CI.

resources.title: Your AI product needs evals (Hamel Husain)

concepts.name: ML Math Foundations
concepts.description: Vectors, matrices, dot products, matmul, shapes.
concepts.mentalModel: Three lenses keep coming back: linear algebra describes the data (vectors and matrices), calculus describes learning (gradients), probability describes uncertainty. Pick the lens that matches the question.

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.description: Loss surfaces, learning rate, SGD steps.
concepts.mentalModel: Gradient descent is repeated tiny adjustments. At each step you move the weights a small amount in the direction that lowers the loss. The "learning rate" is how big each step is — too big and you overshoot the answer, too small and training crawls.

concepts.name: Backpropagation
concepts.mentalModel: Backpropagation is how the network learns: compute the error at the output, then push that error backwards through each layer using the chain rule from calculus. You reuse values from the forward pass so you do not have to recompute them.

concepts.name: Softmax & Cross-Entropy
concepts.description: Logits to probabilities, negative log-likelihood loss.
concepts.mentalModel: Softmax turns raw scores (logits) into probabilities that sum to 1. Cross-entropy then measures how wrong those probabilities are versus the true answer. Together they give a clean gradient that backprop can work with.

concepts.name: AdamW Optimizer
concepts.mentalModel: AdamW is the standard optimizer for training transformers. It adapts the learning rate per parameter (Adam) and applies weight decay separately from the gradient (the "W"). The default choice unless you have a reason to switch.

concepts.name: Tokenization (LLM)
concepts.mentalModel: LLM tokenizers (BPE, SentencePiece) split text into small reusable pieces — often subwords — so the model can handle any input even if it has not seen the exact word. The model and the tokenizer are a pair; swapping one breaks the other.

concepts.name: Language Modeling
concepts.mentalModel: A language model does one thing: predict the next token given the ones before it. Chat, code, reasoning — all of it is built on top of this single objective.

concepts.name: Sampling & Decoding

resources.title: The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration (Holtzman et al.)

concepts.mentalModel: After the model outputs a probability for each next token, you have to pick one. Greedy picks the top — safe but dull. Temperature, top-k, and top-p (nucleus) introduce controlled randomness. Pick by how much variety the task can tolerate.

concepts.name: Embeddings (Transformer)
concepts.mentalModel: Embeddings turn discrete things (words, items, users) into vectors of numbers so that similar things end up close together in space. Distance and direction in that space carry meaning — that geometry is what the model learns from.

concepts.name: Self-Attention
concepts.mentalModel: Self-attention lets every token look at every other token and pull in what is relevant. Each token emits a query, a key, and a value; the query-key dot products (scaled, softmaxed) become weights over the values. Causal masking blocks a token from seeing the future.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling, which destabilizes the softmax
Omitting the causal mask in a decoder, leaking future tokens
Confusing the query/key/value roles

concepts.name: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.description: Parallel heads, head_dim split, output projection.
concepts.mentalModel: Attention lets each token in a sentence look at every other token and weight how relevant each one is. "Multi-head" runs several of these attention passes in parallel so the model can track different kinds of relationships at once (e.g. grammar, references, position).

concepts.name: Transformer Block
concepts.mentalModel: A transformer block is two sublayers — multi-head attention then a position-wise MLP — each wrapped in a residual connection and layer norm. Residuals keep gradients flowing; pre-LayerNorm (norm before the sublayer) makes deep stacks train stably.
commonMistakes: Dropping residual connections and watching deep models fail to train
Post-LayerNorm placement causing instability in deep stacks
Mismatched dimensions between the attention output and the MLP

concepts.name: Training & Debugging
concepts.description: Init, NaNs, overfit tests, gradient checks.

resources.title: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks (Karpathy)

concepts.mentalModel: A training loop is forward pass → loss → backward pass → optimizer step, repeated over batches of data. When training looks broken, the data is the usual culprit (leaked, mislabeled, wrong shape) — check it before blaming the model.

concepts.name: Checkpointing
concepts.description: Weights + optimizer state, resume, dataset manifests.
concepts.mentalModel: A checkpoint saves the weights and optimizer state so you can resume after a crash. Save often enough that any single failure costs less than an hour. Keep the most recent few and your best-eval-score one separately.

concepts.name: LoRA & PEFT
concepts.mentalModel: LoRA fine-tunes a big model cheaply by adding small trainable matrices alongside the frozen weights. You only update around 1% of the parameters yet get most of the quality of full fine-tuning, and you can swap adapters at inference time.

concepts.name: Style Data Engineering
concepts.description: Cleaning, dedup, JSONL tasks, memorization tests.

resources.title: Eugene Yan — Data Flywheels for LLM Applications

concepts.mentalModel: In ML, the dataset is the product. The biggest quality wins usually come from cleaning, deduping, and filtering the data — not from changing the model. Pipelines need versioning and provenance the same way code does.

concepts.name: Browser ML Runtime
concepts.mentalModel: You can run ML in the browser using libraries like ONNX Runtime Web, TensorFlow.js, or Transformers.js. You trade server cost and user privacy for a slower first load and a limit on model size. WebGPU is fastest, WASM works everywhere, CPU is the fallback.

concepts.name: WebGPU Compute
concepts.description: WGSL, compute kernels, matmul, CPU parity.
concepts.mentalModel: WebGPU lets JavaScript run compute shaders on the GPU, which can speed up matrix math 10-100x in the browser. The real bottlenecks are moving data to and from the GPU and compiling the shaders — not the math itself.

concepts.name: Model Evaluation

resources.title: Hamel Husain — Your AI Product Needs Evals

concepts.mentalModel: Offline eval (on a held-out test set) tells you the model got better. Online eval (A/B test on real users) tells you the product got better. You need both — neither alone is enough.

concepts.name: HTTP Lifecycle
concepts.description: DNS → TCP/TLS → request → response: status codes, headers, keep-alive.
concepts.mentalModel: An HTTP request is a journey: resolve DNS, open a TCP connection, negotiate TLS, send the request, the server routes and responds, the connection is reused or closed. Latency and failures hide in each hop.
commonMistakes: Confusing 401 (unauthenticated) with 403 (unauthorized)
Ignoring connection reuse and paying TLS cost per request
Treating all 5xx as retryable and all 4xx as not
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every web service; the substrate under API design.

concepts.name: API Keys
concepts.description: Issuing, hashing, scoping, and rotating keys for machine clients.
concepts.mentalModel: An API key identifies a machine caller. Store only a hash (like a password), show the secret once, attach scopes, and support rotation — because a leaked key is inevitable.
commonMistakes: Storing API keys in plaintext
No prefix, so leaked keys are unsearchable in logs
No rotation or revocation path
concepts.realWorldUsage: Stripe, OpenAI, every developer platform.

concepts.name: Rate Limiting
concepts.description: Token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window.
concepts.mentalModel: Rate limiting protects a shared resource from any one caller. Token bucket is the workhorse: tokens refill at a steady rate, each request spends one, an empty bucket means 429 — allowing bursts up to the bucket size.
commonMistakes: Fixed-window counters that allow 2x burst at the boundary
Limiting per-server instead of globally in a cluster
No Retry-After header on 429 responses
concepts.realWorldUsage: API gateways, every public API.

concepts.name: Idempotency
concepts.description: Idempotency keys and dedup windows for safe retries of mutations.
concepts.mentalModel: Networks make retries inevitable, and retrying a POST can charge a card twice. An idempotency key lets the client say 'this is the same operation' — the server stores the first result and replays it for duplicates.
commonMistakes: Assuming GET-only idempotency and leaving POST unsafe
No expiry on stored idempotency keys
Race between two concurrent requests with the same key
concepts.realWorldUsage: Stripe payments, any mutating API that clients retry.

resources.title: Stripe — Designing robust APIs with idempotency

concepts.name: Retries & DLQ
concepts.description: Backoff + jitter, poison messages, dead-letter queues.
concepts.mentalModel: Retries fix transient failures; backoff with jitter stops a retry storm; a dead-letter queue catches messages that will never succeed so they stop blocking the queue and become debuggable.
commonMistakes: Retrying without backoff and creating a thundering herd
Retrying without jitter so all clients sync up
No DLQ, so one poison message stalls the pipeline
Retrying non-idempotent work and causing double effects
concepts.realWorldUsage: SQS/Kafka consumers, every background job system.

resources.title: Timeouts, retries and backoff with jitter (AWS)

concepts.name: Webhooks
concepts.description: Outbound event delivery: signing, retries, idempotent receivers.
concepts.mentalModel: A webhook is the provider calling your server when something happens, instead of your server polling them. Three rules: verify the signature so you trust the sender, return 200 fast and queue the actual work, and assume the same event may be delivered more than once.

concepts.name: Background Jobs
concepts.description: Offloading slow work to workers: scheduling, concurrency, visibility.

resources.title: Brandur Leach — Transactionally Staged Job Drains

concepts.mentalModel: Background jobs move slow work off the request so the user gets a fast response. The hard parts are idempotency (what if the job runs twice?), retries (transient errors vs permanent ones), and visibility (where did my job go?).

concepts.name: Caching
concepts.mentalModel: A cache trades memory and staleness for speed. The hard parts are not the hit — they are invalidation, the stampede when a hot key expires, and deciding how stale is acceptable.
commonMistakes: Cache-aside with no stampede protection on a hot key
No TTL, so stale data lives forever
Caching per-user data in a shared key
concepts.realWorldUsage: Redis/Memcached, CDN edges, HTTP caching.

concepts.name: Message Queues
concepts.mentalModel: A queue decouples producer speed from consumer speed and turns a sync call into a durable, retryable message. 'Exactly once' is mostly a myth — design for at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers.
commonMistakes: Assuming exactly-once delivery
Non-idempotent consumers on an at-least-once queue
Ignoring ordering guarantees (or their absence)
concepts.realWorldUsage: Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ; ingestion and event pipelines.

concepts.name: Observability
concepts.mentalModel: The three pillars answer different questions: metrics say something is wrong, traces say where, logs say why. Structured logs and a request id stitch them together.
commonMistakes: Unstructured logs you cannot query
Alerting on causes instead of user-facing symptoms (SLOs)
No correlation id across services
concepts.realWorldUsage: OpenTelemetry, Datadog, PostHog, every production service.

concepts.name: API Design
concepts.mentalModel: An API is a contract you cannot easily change. Design for evolution: version it, paginate every list, make errors machine-readable, and keep resources consistent.
commonMistakes: Unpaginated list endpoints that fall over at scale
Breaking changes without a version bump
Inconsistent error shapes across endpoints
concepts.realWorldUsage: Every backend; the Codevetter and HighSignal public surfaces.

concepts.name: Auth Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Authentication proves who you are; authorization decides what you may do. JWTs are stateless and fast but hard to revoke; sessions are revocable but need a store. Pick per the revocation requirement.
commonMistakes: Confusing authentication with authorization
Long-lived JWTs with no revocation strategy
Trusting client-supplied roles
concepts.realWorldUsage: OAuth2/OIDC, every multi-user product.

concepts.name: Payments
concepts.mentalModel: Payment systems are several systems agreeing about money: the card network, the processor, your database, and accounting. Treat every state change like a ledger entry, use idempotency keys on every request, and reconcile against the processor daily.

concepts.name: B-Tree
concepts.description: The balanced, disk-friendly tree behind most relational indexes.
concepts.mentalModel: A B-tree is a wide, shallow tree sized to disk pages: each node holds many keys so the tree stays ~3-4 levels deep even for billions of rows. Reads are fast and in-place; writes do random page updates.
commonMistakes: Confusing B-tree (in-place, read-optimized) with LSM (append, write-optimized)
Ignoring page splits and write amplification
Forgetting that B-trees keep keys sorted, enabling range scans
concepts.realWorldUsage: PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, SQLite — the default index.

concepts.name: LSM Tree
concepts.description: Log-structured merge tree: write-optimized storage via sorted runs.
concepts.mentalModel: An LSM tree turns random writes into sequential ones: buffer writes in an in-memory memtable, flush to immutable sorted files (SSTables), and merge them in the background. Writes are fast; reads may touch several files (bloom filters help).
commonMistakes: Ignoring read amplification across SSTable levels
No bloom filters, so every read scans every file
Forgetting that deletes are tombstones, not removals
concepts.realWorldUsage: RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB, ScyllaDB; the write-heavy storage engine.

concepts.name: Write-Ahead Log
concepts.description: Append-only durability log written before the data pages.
concepts.mentalModel: The WAL is the database's promise: write the change to an append-only log and fsync it before touching the real data structure. A crash mid-write is recoverable by replaying the log. Durability before convenience.
commonMistakes: Acknowledging a write before the WAL is fsync'd
Letting the WAL grow forever with no checkpoint/truncation
Assuming a write() syscall means data is on disk
concepts.realWorldUsage: PostgreSQL WAL, SQLite WAL mode, every durable store and LSM engine.

concepts.name: Compaction
concepts.description: Merging sorted runs to reclaim space and bound read amplification.
concepts.mentalModel: An LSM tree keeps producing sorted files; without compaction reads get slower and deleted data lingers. Compaction merges files, drops tombstones, and bounds read amplification — at the cost of write amplification and IO spikes.
commonMistakes: Ignoring the write-amplification cost of compaction
Not understanding leveled vs size-tiered strategies
Compaction storms that starve foreground traffic
concepts.realWorldUsage: RocksDB/Cassandra compaction strategies; segment merges in search engines.

concepts.name: Object Storage
concepts.description: S3-style blob storage: cheap, durable, high-latency, immutable objects.
concepts.mentalModel: Object storage (S3/R2) is infinite, cheap, durable bytes — but with ~tens-of-ms latency and no in-place edits. Modern systems like Turbopuffer put cold data here and keep a hot cache in front. Storage and compute separate.
commonMistakes: Treating object storage like a low-latency disk
Many tiny objects instead of batched segment files
Ignoring request cost (GETs/PUTs are billed)
concepts.realWorldUsage: S3, Cloudflare R2, GCS; the durable layer of object-storage-first databases.

concepts.name: Columnar Storage
concepts.description: Column-oriented layout for analytics: compression and vectorized scans.
concepts.mentalModel: A column store groups data by column instead of by row. Reading one column out of many becomes very fast (great for analytics), but writing or updating a single row gets slower. Pick row-stores for transactions, column-stores for analytics.

concepts.name: Secondary Indexes
concepts.description: Extra indexes for non-primary-key lookups, and what they cost on writes.
concepts.mentalModel: A secondary index is an extra sorted lookup that maps a non-primary-key value (like email) back to the rows that match. Reads on that value get fast; writes get a little slower because the index also has to update. Every index is a tradeoff — only add the ones queries actually need.

concepts.name: Storage Engines
concepts.mentalModel: A storage engine is the layer that actually puts bytes on disk and finds them again. The defining choice is the write path: B-trees update pages in place (read-optimized), LSM trees append and merge (write-optimized). Everything else — WAL, indexes, caching — serves durability and lookup speed.
commonMistakes: Picking an engine without knowing the read/write ratio of the workload
Ignoring write amplification and its effect on SSD lifetime
Forgetting that every secondary index is extra write cost

concepts.name: Sharding
concepts.mentalModel: Sharding splits one dataset across many machines so it scales past a single box. The shard key is the whole game: hash keys spread load evenly but kill range scans; range keys keep scans but invite hot spots. Re-sharding later is painful, so choose deliberately.
commonMistakes: A shard key that creates a hot shard (e.g. sharding by date)
Cross-shard joins and transactions, which are slow or impossible
No plan for re-sharding as data grows

concepts.name: Replication
concepts.mentalModel: Replication keeps copies of data on multiple nodes for durability and read scaling. Single-leader is simple but the leader is a write bottleneck; multi-leader and leaderless trade simplicity for availability. The core tension is replication lag — followers are always slightly behind.
commonMistakes: Reading from a follower and seeing stale data ("read your own writes" violation)
Assuming failover is instant and lossless
Ignoring replication lag in capacity and correctness planning

concepts.name: CAP & Consistency Models
concepts.mentalModel: During a network partition you must choose: stay Consistent (reject requests that cannot be confirmed) or stay Available (serve possibly-stale data). CAP only bites during a partition; PACELC adds the everyday tradeoff — Else, Latency vs Consistency.
commonMistakes: Thinking you "pick 2 of 3" at all times — the choice only applies during a partition
Calling a system "CA" — partitions are not optional in a distributed system
Ignoring the latency-vs-consistency tradeoff that exists even with no partition

concepts.name: Object Modeling
concepts.mentalModel: Object modeling is finding the small set of "things" the system has (usually 4-7) and the actions that happen between them. Prefer composition (this has-a) over inheritance (this is-a). A good model makes the next requirement easy to add.

concepts.name: State Machines
concepts.mentalModel: A state machine is a fixed set of states plus the rules for moving between them. The win is that illegal transitions become impossible by design — if you find yourself writing many "if status == X and Y" checks, you are simulating a state machine the hard way.

concepts.name: Strategy Pattern
concepts.mentalModel: The strategy pattern lets you swap an algorithm at runtime by hiding it behind a common interface. The caller does not know (or care) which version is used. Reach for it when "the same operation has many implementations" — pricing rules, sort orders, routing strategies.

concepts.name: Observer Pattern
concepts.mentalModel: The observer pattern decouples who emits an event from who listens. Publishers fire events, subscribers register handlers, and the event bus is the registry that connects them. The tradeoff: flows are no longer visible in a single call stack, so debugging gets harder.

concepts.name: Factory & Creational
concepts.mentalModel: Creational patterns (Factory, Builder, Singleton) hide how an object is constructed so callers do not pin themselves to a specific concrete class. They earn their keep when you add the 4th variant without callers needing to change.

concepts.name: Decorator & Structural
concepts.mentalModel: The decorator pattern wraps an object in another object with the same interface, adding behavior without subclassing. It shines when feature combinations explode — instead of one class per combination, you stack small decorators.

concepts.name: Concurrency Design
concepts.mentalModel: Concurrency bugs are usually not "add more locks." They are about having a clear discipline for which locks you take, in what order, and for how long. Often the cheapest fix is a single database transaction or a single-writer design.

concepts.name: Command & Chain
concepts.mentalModel: The command pattern turns an action into a small object you can store, queue, log, or reverse. Pair it with two stacks for undo/redo, with a queue for background jobs, or with a chain for middleware-style processing.

concepts.name: Booking & Inventory
concepts.mentalModel: Booking systems are about "holds" — a reservation is itself a write that blocks other people. Pick aggressive hold expirations, treat overbooking as a business policy (not a bug), and choose optimistic vs pessimistic locking based on how often collisions actually happen.

concepts.name: Game/Simulation Design

resources.title: Hugo Lin — Designing a tic-tac-toe / chess engine

concepts.mentalModel: Realtime game servers run a tight loop over a shared world state. The server is the source of truth; the client predicts what will happen to hide latency, then corrects itself when the server disagrees.

concepts.name: Load Balancing
concepts.mentalModel: A load balancer is the seam between "one address" and "many servers". L4 routes by IP/port (fast, dumb); L7 routes by request content (slower, smart). Health checks and the balancing algorithm (round-robin, least-connections, consistent hashing) decide who gets each request.
commonMistakes: Treating the load balancer as a single point of failure with no redundancy
Round-robin to stateful servers that need sticky sessions
No health checks, so traffic keeps hitting a dead instance

concepts.name: Consistent Hashing
concepts.mentalModel: Consistent hashing maps both servers and keys onto a ring so that when you add or remove a server, only about 1/N of the keys move (instead of nearly all of them). Use "virtual nodes" — multiple ring positions per server — to spread load evenly.

concepts.name: Consensus
concepts.mentalModel: Consensus is how a cluster agrees on one value despite crashes and network delays. Raft makes it understandable: elect a leader, replicate an append-only log, and a write commits once a majority (quorum) has it. A majority guarantees any two quorums overlap.
commonMistakes: Assuming consensus survives a network partition without a majority
Confusing leader election with data replication — Raft does both
Thinking more replicas always means more durability (it costs latency)

concepts.name: Distributed Infra
concepts.mentalModel: Distributed systems multiply the ways things can fail. Every network call can be slow, retried, lost, or duplicated. Design for partial failure first — timeouts, idempotency, bounded queues — and worry about speed second.

concepts.name: Real-time Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Realtime messaging is long-lived connections plus a message fan-out problem. WebSockets are stateful, which constrains your load balancer. How you fan out (per-channel vs per-user) is the central design choice.

concepts.name: Feed Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Feed systems pick between two strategies: precompute each follower's feed when you post (fan-out on write), or assemble it at request time (fan-out on read). The real-world answer is usually hybrid — fan-out-on-write fails for celebrities, fan-out-on-read fails for the long tail.

concepts.name: Streaming Media
concepts.mentalModel: Video streaming is CDN plus adaptive bitrate (HLS or DASH). The origin server should never serve video directly to viewers; the player switches quality on the fly based on the user's bandwidth. Design for the user on bad wifi, not the demo.

concepts.name: Geo Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Geo systems need a spatial index (geohash, S2, quadtree) to answer "nearest" queries quickly. Driver-location updates are write-heavy, so the storage has to handle that. Remember: nearest in distance is not always best in ETA — match on the metric that actually matters.

concepts.name: Collaboration Systems
concepts.mentalModel: Collaborative editors pick one of two conflict-resolution models: Operational Transform (OT), which mathematically rewrites concurrent edits, or CRDTs, which use data structures that merge automatically. CRDTs handle offline well; OT is more compact on the wire. Pick by your latency and offline needs.

concepts.name: Search Platform Design
concepts.description: End-to-end design of a search platform: ingestion, indexing, query, ranking.
concepts.mentalModel: A search platform splits into an ingestion/indexing path (durable, batchy, write-optimized) and a query path (low-latency, cacheable, read-optimized). Segment files, an object-storage tier, and an eval harness tie them together.
commonMistakes: Designing the query path before the indexing path
No segment/compaction story for updates
Forgetting evals as part of the architecture

concepts.name: RAG System Design
concepts.description: Architecting a production RAG system: ingestion, retrieval, generation, evals.
concepts.mentalModel: RAG system design is two pipelines and a contract: an offline ingestion pipeline (chunk → embed → index) and an online query pipeline (retrieve → rerank → pack → generate), bound by an eval harness that gates every change.
commonMistakes: No offline/online separation
No eval gate, so quality regresses silently
Ignoring freshness and re-indexing

concepts.name: Arrays & Hashing
concepts.mentalModel: A hash map trades memory for time: it turns an O(n) scan for "have I seen this?" into an O(1) lookup. Most array problems that feel quadratic collapse to linear once you ask what you would store in a map keyed by value, complement, or frequency.
commonMistakes: Reaching for nested loops before asking "what would a hash map key on?"
Mutating a collection while iterating it
Forgetting hash maps have no order — sort separately if order matters

concepts.name: Two Pointers
concepts.mentalModel: Two pointers replace a nested loop with a single pass by exploiting structure — usually a sorted array or a sequence with a monotonic property. The pointers move toward or away from each other based on a comparison, so each element is visited once.
commonMistakes: Using two pointers on unsorted data when the technique needs order
Off-by-one errors at the start/end conditions
Moving the wrong pointer and skipping valid pairs

concepts.name: Sliding Window
concepts.mentalModel: A sliding window maintains a contiguous range and an aggregate (sum, count, max) as it moves. Expand the right edge to include elements; shrink the left edge when a constraint breaks. Each element enters and leaves the window at most once — O(n).
commonMistakes: Recomputing the aggregate from scratch instead of updating incrementally
Shrinking with an `if` when a `while` is needed to restore the invariant
Confusing fixed-size and variable-size window templates

concepts.name: Stack
concepts.mentalModel: A stack is last-in-first-out: the last thing you pushed is the first thing you pop. Reach for one when the natural question is "what was the most recent unfinished thing?" — parsing, recursion, undo, matching brackets.

concepts.name: Binary Search
concepts.description: Halving sorted search space, predicate search.
concepts.mentalModel: Binary search is not about sorted arrays — it is about a monotonic predicate. If you can phrase the answer as "the smallest x for which condition(x) is true", you can binary-search the answer space, halving it each step.
commonMistakes: Infinite loops from a wrong mid update or loop condition
Searching values when you should search the answer space
Integer overflow in `(lo + hi) / 2` — use `lo + (hi - lo) / 2`

concepts.name: Linked List
concepts.mentalModel: A linked list trades fast random access for fast inserts and deletes wherever you already have a pointer. The classic moves: a dummy head to simplify edge cases, two pointers (fast and slow), and reversing in place.

concepts.name: Trees
concepts.mentalModel: A tree is recursion made concrete: almost every tree problem is "solve it for the children, then combine". DFS (pre/in/post-order) is a recursion or an explicit stack; BFS is a queue, level by level. Pick the traversal that matches what you need to combine.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the null/empty base case
Using DFS when the problem is about levels or shortest depth (use BFS)
Returning the wrong thing from recursion — separate "answer" from "info passed up"

concepts.name: Trie
concepts.mentalModel: A trie stores strings in a tree where each path from the root spells out a prefix. It makes prefix queries (autocomplete, IP routing) take time proportional to the length of the prefix, not the size of the dataset.

concepts.name: Heap / Priority Queue
concepts.mentalModel: A heap gives you the min or max in O(1) and re-balances in O(log n). Whenever a problem says "top k", "k-th largest", or "merge k sorted", a heap of size k is usually the answer — far cheaper than sorting everything.
commonMistakes: Sorting the whole input when a size-k heap suffices
Using a max-heap when top-k smallest needs a max-heap of size k (and vice versa)
Forgetting most languages give a min-heap by default

concepts.name: Backtracking
concepts.mentalModel: Backtracking is "try a choice, recurse, undo if it does not work, try the next one." It explores all possibilities, but you cut whole branches by stopping early when you can prove they will never reach a valid answer.

concepts.name: Graphs
concepts.mentalModel: Most graph problems are one of a few templates: reachability/components (DFS/BFS), shortest path (BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra for weighted), ordering (topological sort), or connectivity (union-find). The skill is recognizing the template and modeling the input as nodes and edges.
commonMistakes: No visited set — revisiting nodes and looping forever
Using DFS for shortest path on an unweighted graph instead of BFS
Not handling disconnected components

concepts.name: Shortest Path
concepts.mentalModel: Pick the algorithm by the graph: BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra when weights are non-negative, Bellman-Ford if weights can be negative, A* when you have a heuristic estimating distance to the goal. Using the wrong one gives wrong answers or wasted time.

concepts.name: Union-Find
concepts.mentalModel: Union-Find tracks which items are in the same group with two operations: union(a, b) merges two groups, find(a) returns the group id. With path compression and union by rank, both run in essentially constant time. Use it whenever the question is "are these two in the same group?"

concepts.name: 1D DP
concepts.mentalModel: Dynamic programming is recursion with the repeated work cached. Define the state (what does dp[i] mean?), the recurrence (how does dp[i] build on earlier states?), and the base case. 1D DP means the state is a single index along a sequence.
commonMistakes: Starting to code before clearly defining what dp[i] represents
Wrong iteration order so a state is read before it is computed
Missing or wrong base cases

concepts.name: 2D DP
concepts.mentalModel: 2D dynamic programming fills a table where each cell depends on a few neighbors (often the ones above and to the left). The hard part is naming the state precisely — once dp[i][j] has a clear one-sentence meaning, the recurrence usually falls out.

concepts.name: Greedy
concepts.mentalModel: A greedy algorithm always picks the choice that looks best right now. It only gives the right answer when the problem has a structure (an exchange argument) that proves local choices add up correctly. When they do not, greedy quietly returns wrong answers.

concepts.name: Intervals
concepts.mentalModel: Interval problems almost always start by sorting — usually by start time, sometimes by end. After sorting, a single pass with a couple of running variables (last end, current count) handles merge, overlap, scheduling, and room allocation.

concepts.name: Math & Geometry
concepts.mentalModel: Interviews use math sparingly: gcd/lcm, modular arithmetic, primes, combinatorics, and basic geometry (cross product, line intersection). Recognize the shape of the problem — the formula is a quick lookup away.

concepts.name: Bit Manipulation
concepts.mentalModel: Bits give you three superpowers: a bitmask is an O(1) set, XOR cancels duplicates, and checking power-of-2 is a single AND. Two tricks worth memorizing: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit, and XOR-ing equal values gives zero.

concepts.name: Leadership
concepts.mentalModel: Leadership without authority is getting people aligned on an outcome when you cannot order them to do it. The real signal is sustained behavior change in others over weeks — not the meeting where they nodded along.

concepts.name: Conflict Resolution
concepts.mentalModel: Conflict means information surfaced — two people disagree about something that was probably already there. Resolution is naming the disagreement clearly, finding the shared goal underneath, and choosing a path forward. It is not the same as winning.

concepts.name: Decision Making
concepts.mentalModel: Decisions are bets you make with incomplete information. Judge them by the reasoning at the time, not the outcome. Good decisions name in advance what evidence would tell you you are wrong.

concepts.name: Collaboration
concepts.mentalModel: Collaboration means making the other person's job easier than they were expecting. Most cross-team friction is really unclear ownership and unspoken expectations — fix those before worrying about chemistry.

concepts.name: Failure & Learning
concepts.mentalModel: A real failure cost something you cannot get back. A useful failure story names exactly what you got wrong, what you tried next, and what concretely changed in your behavior since. "I learned a lot" is not a lesson.

concepts.name: Communication
concepts.mentalModel: Good communication is audience-first: after listening, the other person's next decision should be easier. Be specific, not sophisticated. One sharp analogy beats five vague ones.

concepts.name: Prioritization
concepts.mentalModel: Prioritization is saying no to good things, not bad ones. If you cannot list what you are NOT doing this week, you have not actually prioritized — you have just written a wish list.

concepts.name: Innovation
concepts.mentalModel: Most real innovation comes from refusing to accept a tradeoff that everyone else is taking for granted. The signal is not novelty for its own sake — it is finding the assumption you can credibly weaken.

concepts.name: Customer Obsession
concepts.mentalModel: Customer obsession is not saying yes to whatever they ask. It is taking their underlying problem seriously, sometimes by shipping something they did not ask for. One customer's complaint is a hypothesis, not proof — go check whether it generalizes.

concepts.name: Ownership
concepts.mentalModel: Ownership means closing the loop, not playing the hero. The opposite of ownership is "that is not my team." Real ownership notices the moments you could have said that and chose not to.

concepts.name: Positioning
concepts.description: Naming the problem, the audience, and the alternative you beat.
concepts.mentalModel: Positioning answers two questions: "for whom" and "compared to what alternative." If your statement could just as well describe three of your competitors, you have a description — not positioning.

concepts.name: Landing Pages
concepts.description: Turning a visitor into an activated user: hero, proof, single CTA.
concepts.mentalModel: A landing page has one job: get the right visitor to take one action. Headline that says what it is and who it is for, one clear call to action, social proof, no distractions. If a visitor cannot tell what the product does in 3 seconds, you have lost them.

concepts.name: SEO
concepts.description: Programmatic and content SEO: intent, structure, indexability.
concepts.mentalModel: SEO is matching what people are searching for and earning the trust to rank for it through good content and credible links. On-page basics (title, H1, page speed) are necessary but not sufficient — the real fight is content depth and inbound links.

concepts.name: Product Analytics
concepts.description: Activation, retention, funnels — measuring whether the product works.
concepts.mentalModel: Good analytics looks like a small tree: one north-star metric that captures the value users get, two or three input metrics that drive it, and one guardrail you must not regress on. If a PM cannot make a tradeoff from this tree alone, the tree is wrong.
